title: Chapter 12: Those about to Die
paragraphs: The Eastern extraction mine of Agdash turned into a boiling, surreal cauldron of hell. The portal at the bottom of the pit was not just a hole in space; it was a living, pulsating whirlpool of necrosis that spewed the underside of the universe into the real world. The air above the hopper was distorted beyond recognition, the gravity density changed chaotically, and from the crimson maw, a never-ending stream of screaming, multi-jointed void creatures poured out. Elven miners in panic discarded their picks, trying to climb wooden scaffolding that collapsed under the weight of monsters with a crash.
When the Isekai squad reached the edge of the pit, it was one thing to hear about the fatalism of the universe, and quite another to look the rotting end of the world right in the throat.
"Yura is there!" Alexander shouted, pointing down over the deafening roar of the portal.

Their friend wasn't hiding in the dust. The former office worker, dressed in a miner's jacket stained with ore dust, stood on a sturdy rock ledge above the inferno. He didn't wave at the creatures in blind panic. He methodically commanded. His voice, amplified by a megaphone made from an iron pipe, broke through the chaos with force: he built an evacuation route, forcing the frightened elves not to crowd at the narrow lifts, but to descend in streams along the reserve ramps. Yuri, without a single drop of magic, defeated entropy with his main and only weapon—iron organization and order.
But several large portal reaper-worms broke through the thin cordon of guards and rushed directly toward the foreman's ledge.

Ravan did not hesitate for a second. The dentist jumped from a height of five meters, landing with a heavy thud directly between Yuri and the creatures. The pneumatic armor-gauntlet hissed maliciously, venting magical steam. When a huge worm rushed at him, Ravan drove his steel fist, amplified by a demonic piston, directly into its chitinous face. The piston struck with such crushing force that the monster's head simply exploded into a black-green fountain, and the ten-ton carcass was thrown back. Ravan roared—a roar not of fear, but of primal, heady happiness. He had again become the protector of his pack.

At that moment, creatures trying to go behind Ravan and Yuri's backs began to fall dead from the upper tier with chilling precision. This was Alexander, who had coolly raised his new mag-crossbow, as if in a shooting range, shooting the monsters. His finger smoothly, as in training, pulled the trigger, sending armor-piercing bolts into flight.

Anar, standing next to Sasha, closed his eyes. He was too lazy to run down. But he had to protect his own. Drawing the dirty air into his chest, he struck with both hands. A blinding-blue, branching cascade of chain lightning hit the bottom of the mine, incinerating small parasites by the dozens. The air filled with thunderclaps and the sharp smell of burnt ozone.

Avotu drew Nature's Blade, his black flame flared brightly, reacting to his internal storm. He jumped into the thick of enemies, crashing into the ranks of monsters like a black meteorite, grinding flesh and burning shells. But inside the elf felt an icy, dead cold. There were too many creatures. The portal was expanding. No matter how many they killed today, twice as many would come tomorrow. His PTSD screamed: "Again. You won't save anyone again. They will all die, like Biotrix died." The meaning of his struggle was flowing through his fingers.

The ancient stones of Agdash trembled. With a deafening sound of golden war horns, warriors encased in heavy armor came out onto the edges of the mine. The King's personal guard. And in front of them, leaning heavily on a staff carved from a dragon's bone, walked Agsham himself. An old man who had spoken in the palace that very morning about the inevitability of death.

The eyes of the decrepit king burned not with physical fire, but with absolute, fanatical will. He raised both dry hands to the torn heavens. The old man scooped mana from the deepest, inviolable reserves of his fading life. A river of roaring, blinding-white flame tore from his hands and hit the center of the portal. Pure plasma suppressed the spatial rupture for several precious minutes, burning out creatures at the root, giving time to pull out the wounded and tightly block the passage with collapsed rock.

When the white flame died out, the king sank to one knee, exhausted. Avotu was next to him with one jump, supporting the old man by the shoulders. The strangers, breathing heavily, leaning on their weapons and covered with the stinking ichor of monsters, huddled around them.
Agsham slowly raised his muddy eyes to the people from another world and the broken great hero. The old man spat blood.

"You asked me in the palace why we fight if reality is doomed and the counter is started," the old king croaked. His look, full of deep sadness, circled the ruined mine, the rescued workers, and five friends from another cursed world who stood shoulder to shoulder. "The answer is before you."

The king chuckled: "The point is not to save the world, Avotu. That is an illusion for fairy tales. Worlds are born and die. The point is... **how** we meet this end. We do not plead for mercy. We do not abandon our own in the darkness. While you stand, shielding your brother's back—you prove to the void that your life had a price."

Alexander firmly, until it crunched, squeezed Ravan's shoulder, Zhenya encouragingly hugged Yuri, and Anar silently, with approval, nodded to them. Avotu looked at these people. They were not immortal heroes. They were just workers, doctors, and managers who had been torn from home into hell. But right now, in their eyes burned a fire much brighter and more important than ancient magic.
